---
title: "Day 25 — Sweep the knobs, then sweep the decoder"
subtitle: "Monday, 17 August 2026 · Week 4 · Build a GPT · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 24](day-24.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 26 →](day-26.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
You now have a model that trains in minutes and six hyperparameters you have never turned, which is the cheapest opportunity you will ever get to find out what each of them actually does.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You can point at the step where val loss turns upward on your dropout=0.0 run while train loss keeps falling, and say why the dropout=0.2 run does not do that.
- You can say what temperature 0.5 and top-k=1 do differently to a sample, given that both make it more conservative.
- You can report which single hyperparameter change bought the most val loss per second of compute on your hardware, with the table row to back it.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>215 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [30 min]{.task-min} Refactor `gpt_mine.py` so the configuration is a dict or argparse namespace and one function `run(cfg)` returns the train and val curves plus wall-clock seconds. Thirteen runs from the command line beats thirteen hand-edited copies of the file, and it stops you from misattributing a result to the wrong config.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Run a one-factor-at-a-time sweep off a fixed baseline (n_layer=4, n_head=4, n_embd=128, block_size=64, lr=1e-3, dropout=0.0, 1500 steps), varying block_size over {16, 64, 256}, n_layer over {1, 4, 8}, n_head over {1, 4, 8}, n_embd over {64, 128, 384}, lr over {1e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3}, dropout over {0.0, 0.2, 0.5}. Log train and val loss every 100 steps to CSV. The full grid is 729 runs; you are doing 13 and you should be able to say what that costs you.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Plot the curves, one panel per swept factor, train and val on the same axes with distinguishable line styles. Label the y-axis 'cross-entropy loss (nats per character)', not 'loss'. Mark the step where each val curve turns up.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Sweep decoding on your single best checkpoint: temperature over {0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.3} with top-k off, then top-k over {1, 10, 50, None} at temperature 1.0. Same seed, same 20-character prompt, 200 tokens each.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Write `experiments.md` with one table for the sweep (config, parameter count, final train loss, final val loss, seconds) and one for decoding (setting, a 20-token excerpt, one sentence of judgement). End with the single sentence: the change that bought the most val loss per second was X, worth Y nats for Z seconds.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [Weights & Biases](https://wandb.ai/site) [tool]{.res-kind}
- [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/) [tool]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
`experiments.md` with the 13-run sweep table, the six curve panels exported as a PNG with labelled axes and units, and the decoding table with excerpts. A colleague should be able to read the tables and pick a config without running anything.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
What does temperature approaching 0 reduce the sampler to, and what happens to your Shakespeare samples after roughly 100 tokens of it? You have the sample in front of you - check whether your answer matches it.
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 26, Tuesday, 18 August 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Clone nanoGPT with `git clone https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT` and run `pip install -r requirements.txt` tonight - tiktoken and datasets are not instant installs.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Run `python data/shakespeare_char/prepare.py` so tomorrow's training starts with no download in front of it.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Run `wc -l model.py train.py` and note the two numbers. Do not read the files yet.
:::
:::
